title: An Adaptive QoS and Trust-Based Lightweight Secure Routing Algorithm for WSNs

sections.name: Body
sections.content:
23826 IEEE INTERNET OF THINGS JOURNAL, VOL. 9, NO. 23, 1 DECEMBER 2022
An Adaptive QoS and Trust-Based Lightweight
Secure Routing Algorithm for WSNs
Aditya Pathak , Graduate Student Member, IEEE , Irfan Al-Anbagi , Senior Member, IEEE,
and Howard J. Hamilton
Abstract—The limited resources and low computational power
of wireless sensor networks (WSNs) make them vulnerable to various security attacks. Conventional security mechanisms require
too many resources to allow the reliable operation of WSNs due to
their resource-constrained nature. In addition, multihop communication in WSNs creates a requirement for guaranteed Quality
of Service (QoS). Therefore, providing security while maintaining QoS and energy efﬁciency in WSNs are important design
considerations. To further increase the performance of WSNs,
there is a need to overcome the energy-hole problem, which
leads to poor coverage of the ﬁeld of interest. An energy-hole
problem is created because of using poor deployment strategies.
In this article, we deﬁne a multiobjective WSN optimization
problem and present a novel algorithm known as lightweight
secure routing (LSR) to manage WSNs that directly addresses the
multiobjective WSN optimization problem. Our LSR algorithm
uses ant colony optimization (ACO), an adaptive security model
based on direct and indirect trust calculations, an adaptive QoS
model, a hybrid deployment model based on 2-D Gaussian and
uniform distributions, and an adaptive connectivity model that
uses an appropriate communicational radius to ensure high connectivity between sensor nodes to solve the multiobjective WSN
optimization problem. We divide our simulation results into three
analyses, namely, trust model analysis, network scalability analysis, and security risk analysis to show that LSR outperforms
the existing techniques in terms of energy consumed to calculate
trust values, trust values convergence, network lifetime, average
routing delay, and packet delivery ratio.
Index Terms —Ant colony optimization (ACO), energy efﬁciency, energy-hole problem, lightweight security, multiobjective
wireless sensor network (WSN) optimization problem, network
security, Quality of Service (QoS), trust model, WSNs.
I. I NTRODUCTION
T
HE DYNAMIC nature and simple operation of wireless sensor networks (WSNs) make them an attractive
choice for many Internet of Things (IoT) applications. The
number of sensors deployed in WSNs increased from 0.45 to
2 billion between 2012 and 2020 [ 1]. However, the simplicity
Manuscript received 2 June 2022; accepted 30 June 2022. Date of publication 11 July 2022; date of current version 21 November 2022. This work was
supported by ISM Canada. The work of Howard J. Hamilton was supported
by the Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada through
the Collaborative Research and Development Grant. (Corresponding author:
Irfan Al-Anbagi.)
Aditya Pathak and Irfan Al-Anbagi are with the Faculty of Engineering and
Applied Science, University of Regina, Regina, SK S4S 0A2, Canada (e-mail:
apa142@uregina.ca; irfan.al-anbagi@uregina.ca).
Howard J. Hamilton is with the Department of Computer Science,
University of Regina, Regina, SK S4S 0A2, Canada (e-mail: hamilton@
cs.uregina.ca).
Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/JIOT.2022.3189832
of WSNs limits the available resources in the nodes, which
imposes challenges for algorithm design and WSN security.
Due to these design challenges, existing WSNs are vulnerable
to security attacks. First, the wireless nature of communication allows eavesdropping on trafﬁc exchanged in the WSN,
making the nodes prone to several types of security attacks.
Second, the sensor nodes have limited computing capabilities,
limited communication bandwidth, and low storage capacity
due to their small size and limited energy. Third, in remote
WSN applications, such as environmental monitoring, healthcare monitoring, and battleﬁeld surveillance, the nodes can be
easily captured and compromised, which threatens the viability of their cryptography-based security algorithms. Overall,
security is a crucial challenge for current WSNs because disruptive and malicious actions are able to compromise their
functioning to a signiﬁcant extent [ 1]–[3].
Due to the above limitations, it is not easy to directly
employ conventional cryptography-based security solutions in
WSNs [1]. Most conventional security models defend against
external attacks but not internal ones. Since the network cannot differentiate between compromised and legitimate nodes,
network performance, such as routing delay, network lifetime,
etc, are degraded [ 3]. In past, key management algorithms [ 4]
and user authentication schemes [3] have been used to achieve
security in WSNs. However, they are ineffective against
internal attacks and require more computation to secure WSNs.
Thus, energy efﬁciency decreases and sometimes Quality of
Service (QoS) constraints, such as minimal delay (required to
support real-time operation) are not achieved. Overall, security is increased at the expense of higher energy consumption
and longer delays. This type of tradeoff is not suitable for
resource-constrained networks like WSNs. Therefore, security
models that mitigate internal attacks and provide energyefﬁcient operation while satisfying QoS constraints are needed
for WSNs [ 2].
One approach to detect and defend against internal attacks,
and also satisfy the above requirements of energy efﬁciency
and QoS, is to base security in a WSN on a trust model [ 5].
A trust model speciﬁes how a node can evaluate the trustworthiness of other nodes, using information present at the node
(direct trust) or gathered from other nodes ( indirect trust)[ 6].
Trust models with direct and indirect trust calculations provide
higher security than those with only direct trust calculations
because indirect trust methods can isolate the activity from
compromised nodes much faster than direct trust methods and
select secure routes [ 7]. However, calculating indirect trust
2327-4662 c⃝ 2022 IEEE. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission.
See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.
Authorized licensed use limited to: University of Regina IEL. Downloaded on June 16,2025 at 15:34:17 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply.

PATHAK et al.: ADAPTIVE QoS AND TRUST-BASED LIGHTWEIGHT SECURE ROUTING ALGORITHM FOR WSNs 23827
values in WSNs is an energy-consuming process, and the
resource constraints of WSNs preclude the calculation of high
levels of indirect trust. Therefore, to provide security in an
energy-efﬁcient manner, a trust model should be lightweight,
i.e., it should require few calculations.
In addition to the above challenges, nodes near the base
station (BS) of the WSN consume their energy much faster
than other nodes because these nodes relay all the trafﬁc from
the other nodes to BS. This is known as the energy-hole
problem [8]. The energy-hole problem imposes fundamental
design constraints on WSNs, including a tradeoff between coverage and network lifetime. Coverage refers to the fraction of
the monitored area that is accurately represented by the collected data. Network lifetime refers to the length of time (in
rounds) for which the WSN is effective at collecting sensor
data [8]. Here, a round is the interval during which an event
is detected and the event data is transmitted from the source
node to BS. The energy-hole problem can cause BS to become
detached from the remainder of the WSN, which reduces the
network’s lifetime, QoS, and security.
To promote the widespread adoption of secure WSNs,
multiple objectives must be addressed. The multiobjective
WSN optimization problem is the problem of designing a
WSN that mitigates the energy-hole problem, provides high
coverage, and jointly optimizes energy efﬁciency, QoS, and
security. This article is the ﬁrst to formulate this multiobjective
problem.
To solve this problem, we propose a lightweight secure routing (LSR) algorithm, a novel algorithm for managing WSNs
that directly addresses the multiobjective WSN optimization
problem. Our algorithm achieves that by integrating four new
models. First, our adaptive QoS model enhances QoS and
energy efﬁciency by improving the selection of the source
node. Second, our adaptive security model improves security
using a trust model that calculates direct and indirect trust
values. Third, our adaptive connectivity model mitigates the
energy-hole problem in low node density networks by improving the connectivity between sensor nodes. Finally, our hybrid
deployment model uses the Gaussian and uniform distribution
deployment to mitigate the energy-hole problem for high node
density networks and provides high coverage. The LSR algorithm does not negatively affect the network lifetime of sensor
nodes. We divide our simulation results into three analyses,
namely, trust model analysis, network scalability analysis, and
security risk analysis, to show that LSR outperforms existing techniques in terms of energy consumption for calculating
trust values, trust value convergence, network lifetime, average
routing delay, and packet delivery ratio.
Our main novel contributions are summarized as follows.
1) We propose an adaptive QoS model to enhance QoS
and energy efﬁciency of the network by improving the
selection of a source node. We measure QoS using average routing delay and PDR, and energy efﬁciency using
network lifetime.
2) We propose an adaptive security model to improve
network security by incorporating a trust model. We
measure security using average routing delay and
PDR. Also, we measure the energy consumption for
calculating trust values as well as the rate of convergence for the trust values in order to provide evidence
that our trust model is lightweight and secure.
3) We propose an adaptive connectivity model that mitigates the energy-hole problem for low node density
networks by improving connectivity between sensor
nodes. We measure the performance of this model using
network lifetime, average routing delay, and PDR.
4) We propose a hybrid deployment model that improves
coverage in WSNs and mitigates the energy-hole
problem for high node density networks. We measure
the performance of this model using network lifetime,
average routing delay, and PDR.
The remainder of this article is organized as follows. In
Section II, we summarize related work. In Section III, we discuss our network models and assumptions. In Section IV , we
introduce the LSR algorithm. In Section V , we present simulation results and analysis. Finally, in Section VI, we conclude
this article.
II. R
ELATED WORK
Security in a WSN is achieved either by data encryption and
key management or by identifying and removing compromised
nodes. Abdmeziem and Tandjaoui [ 4] have proposed a key
management algorithm for providing end-to-end security for ehealth applications. Liu and Chung [ 3] have proposed a secure
user authentication scheme for WSNs. However, to establish
secure communications, we need to ensure that all communicating nodes are trusted. Thus, it is critical to choose a trust
model that allows a sensor node to infer the trustworthiness
of another node.
Dhanalakshmi et al. [9] have proposed a secure and energyefﬁcient routing protocol for WSNs using fuzzy logic and
node’s trust values to improve the packet delivery ratio. The
protocol can address challenges, such as node mobility, energy
balancing, and energy optimization by predicting an optimal
communication path in the network. Fang et al. [10]h a v e
proposed a Gaussian distribution-based comprehensive trust
management system (GDTMS) for industrial WSNs. Greybased decision-making theory selects the most reliable packet
forwarding neighbor node based on trust values and the number of hops as input. The simulation results show that GDTMS
achieves a good tradeoff between security, energy, and transmission performance (throughput). Rodrigues and John [ 11]
have proposed a trust-based routing algorithm named Joint
Trust to ﬁnd a secure routing path using parameters, such as
path integrity factor, path consistency factor, forwarding rate,
and path availability factor. A ChickenDragonﬂy (CHicDra)
optimization algorithm is used to ﬁnd optimal cluster heads
for secure communication.
Ademaj and Bernhard [ 12] have proposed a QoS-based
minimal routing protocol for WSNs using a time-division
multiple access scheme. The routing protocol can minimize
end-to-end delay, provide balanced power consumption, and
improve energy efﬁciency by avoiding packet collisions in
the network. Abuzainab et al. [13] have proposed a QoS
Authorized licensed use limited to: University of Regina IEL. Downloaded on June 16,2025 at 15:34:17 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply.

23828 IEEE INTERNET OF THINGS JOURNAL, VOL. 9, NO. 23, 1 DECEMBER 2022
and jamming-aware routing protocol to ensure robust communication against eavesdropping and jamming attacks using
deep reinforcement learning. The protocol can maximize the
network performance in terms of QoS, security, and energy
efﬁciency. Rastogi et al. [14] have proposed one such hybrid
optimization algorithm that uses ant colony optimization
(ACO) and particle swarm optimization (PSO) to provide reliable routing, which enhances the network lifetime of WSNs.
However, the algorithm does not consider the effect of compromised nodes and no security parameter is involved in the
calculation of routing paths.
Li and Song [ 15] have proposed an attack-resistant trust
management algorithm to cope with malicious vehicles in
vehicular networks. The trustworthiness of a vehicle is calculated based on functional trust and recommendation trust.
Zhang et al. [16] have proposed a trust management algorithm
using artiﬁcial intelligence (AI) and the blockchain technology
to address security challenges in vehicular networks. A deep
learning algorithm is used to establish and manage the trust
values of vehicles, and the blockchain technology is used to
validate the identities of the vehicles in the network. However,
both algorithms ignore QoS considerations, which are highly
relevant to vehicular networks.
Existing WSN designs have varying levels of effectiveness
with respect to the energy-hole problem. Mohemed et al. [8]
have proposed two routing protocols known as on-hole children reconnection (OHCR) and on-hole alert (OHA) to mitigate the energy-hole problem in WSNs. Lipare et al. [17]
have proposed an energy-efﬁcient routing structure using
a multilayer network model consisting of the clustering
scheme to mitigate the energy-hole problem around BS.
Roselin et al. [18] have proposed an energy-efﬁcient connected coverage (EECC) scheduling to increase the network
lifetime and mitigate the energy-hole problem. Previous methods [8], [17], [18] for solving the energy-hole problem require
extensive computation on the sensor nodes and do not consider the effect of compromised nodes in their network.
For instance, a ﬂooding attack can decrease the energy of
nearby nodes giving rise to an energy-hole problem. Thus,
it proves ineffective for WSNs scenarios. One promising solution to the energy-hole problem is to deploy more sensors
close to BS, which is possible using the Gaussian distribution deployment method, with BS as the center point [ 21].
However, this method decreases the coverage area of sensor nodes in the Field of Interest (FoI). Luo et al. [19]
have proposed an algorithm to increase the coverage area
in FoI known as the maximum coverage sets scheduling
(MCSS) algorithm using a greedy approach. However, the
authors do not consider the effect of compromised nodes
in the network and do not solve the energy-hole problem.
Therefore, it is important to mitigate the energy-hole problem,
increase the coverage area in the FoI, and consider the
effect of compromised nodes in the network at the same
time.
Rathee et al. [20] have proposed the QoS-aware energy balancing secure routing (QEBSR) algorithm, which uses ACO.
QEBSR currently provides a good solution to the problem
of simultaneous optimization of energy efﬁciency, QoS, and
security. However, four shortcomings should be addressed to
improve the network’s effectiveness.
1) The effects of energy-hole and coverage problems are
not addressed because of QEBSR’s uniform deployment
model. It results in a low network lifetime.
2) The trust model is limited to direct trust values, which
reduces the mitigation of compromised nodes and
increases energy consumption in the network due to frequent packet drops and retransmissions. It results in a
lower network lifetime and compromised security in the
network.
3) There is unbalanced energy consumption at the nodes
during the process of selecting the source node. This
results in higher energy consumption for a few nodes
and ultimately reduces the network lifetime and QoS.
4) The QEBSR algorithm has not been validated using
a network simulator, making it difﬁcult to analyze its
performance when deployed in a real environment.
According to our related work (summarized in Table I),
previous researchers have addressed various parts of the
multiobjective WSN optimization problem. However, to
improve the performance of WSNs signiﬁcantly, one needs to
solve the entire multiobjective WSN optimization problem as a
whole, i.e., the algorithm must be able to mitigate the energyhole problem, provide high coverage, and jointly optimize
energy efﬁciency, QoS, and security. In addition, the approach
of Rathee et al. [20], while promising, has the four shortcomings listed above. Therefore, in this article, we address
these four shortcomings and also provide a novel and effective
solution to the multiobjective WSN optimization problem.
III. N
ETWORK MODELS AND ASSUMPTIONS
In this section, we introduce our network models and
assumptions.
A. WSN Deployment Model
We consider a WSN network in a 2-D plane that consists of
a BS node and N homogeneous sensor nodes in a square FoI
with side length L and area A = L2. We assume that the nodes
of a WSN are randomly deployed in the FoI, e.g., by dropping
from an airplane or moving vehicles. Thus, the locations of
the nodes follow a Gaussian distribution [ 22].
For deploying nodes, we propose a hybrid deployment
model based on a hybrid distribution that comprises a Gaussian
distribution (i.e., the normal distribution) and four uniform
distributions. The probability distribution function (PDF) of
the Gaussian distribution that deploys the sensor nodes in the
FoI is
f(x, y) = 1

We assume that BS is placed at the center of FoI, and so the
deployment point is set to the coordinates of BS. Rectangular
strips are deﬁned inside the FoI along its four outer edges,
as shown in Fig. 1. The PDF of the uniform distribution that

Our novel hybrid deployment model improves the coverage area in the FoI, and mitigates the energy-hole problem
when node density is high. Fig. 1 shows a sample deployment
in our simulation using our hybrid deployment method. The
Gaussian distribution model ensures extra nodes are deployed
near BS to overcome the energy-hole problem. However, a
Gaussian distribution model alone tends to have poor coverage of the outer edges [ 7]. Therefore, it is used to deploy
60% of the total number of nodes. The uniform distribution model is used to deploy the remaining 40% of the
nodes, to provide high coverage around the edges of the
FoI. The uniform components of the model provide high
Fig. 1. Hybrid deployment.
coverage by deploying some nodes near the corners of the FoI.
The values of 60% and 40% were chosen after preliminary
experimentation.

The adaptive connectivity model is a novel model that
ensures high connectivity between the nodes by using an
appropriate communication radius (R

Our adaptive connectivity model (3) enforces a relation

to ensure full connectivity. This second case applied to low
node density networks, because the value of RC depends on
the number of nodes. Thus, the adaptive connectivity model
helps mitigate the energy-hole problem for low node density
networks.
C. Communication Model
We use the disk communication model [ 23] to represent
multihop communication between the nodes. The model states
that any two sensors i and j, located at (xi, yi) and (xj, yj),
respectively, can only communicate with each other if the
Euclidean distance between them is less than R

related to residual energy, energy required for transmitting
data to BS, generated packets, received packets, transmitted
packets, queue delay, trust values, and the locations of its
neighbors.
D. Energy Model
We consider a lightweight ﬁrst-order energy model, assuming that the energy cost for data communication is much higher
than that for computation and sensing. We do not consider
the latter energy cost in this article. We assume that a sensor
node is limited in energy and disconnects from the network
once its energy is exhausted. We assume BS is connected to
an energy source. The energy consumption calculation relies
on the distance between the communicating nodes [ 24]. The
transmission energy cost for m bits of information for distance
d is
E

bits over distance d, Eelec is the energy required to activate the
transmitter or receiver circuitry, ϵf is the energy required for
the ampliﬁer circuit in the free space communication model,
ϵm is the energy required for the ampliﬁer circuit in the
multipath communication model, and dth = √(ϵf /ϵm) is the
threshold distance used to choose the path loss model.
The receiver energy cost for m bits of information is
ERX(m) = Eelec ∗m. (8)
Communication in a WSN is modeled as a series of
rounds [20]. In every round, an event is generated and reported
to BS via several sensor nodes, each with an initial energy, E0.
To track the energy consumption in the network, we calculate
the amount of residual energy ERE after each round of operations for all nodes. We assume that a node generates and

E. Adaptive QoS Model
Our proposed adaptive QoS model improves the selection
of source nodes to transmit the information related to event
detection. Here, QoS is achieved by lower latency (i.e., average
routing delay) and higher PDR. To implement the model, ﬁrst
we use the following binary detection model [ 25] to describe
the reporting of events to BS:

between event Hi and node j.

NR(j) yields the ratio between the number
of uncompromised neighbors to the total number of
neighbors for a given node j. This factor increases the
preference for a source node with more trustworthy
neighbors, which reduces delays and retransmissions due
to routing attacks in the network.
F . Adaptive Security Model
Our proposed adaptive security model makes use of direct
and indirect trust calculations to mitigate the effect of compromised nodes proactively, unlike the most similar previous
approach, which uses only direct trust calculations [ 20]. In
our model, we consider two types of routing attacks. In a
Blackhole attack [26], a compromised node drops all received
packets during the routing process. In a Flooding attack [27],
a compromised node ﬂoods the network with packets. These
attacks reduce network lifetime and PDR, and also increase
routing delays in the network. In this article, a compromised
node is deﬁned as a node that is affected by the attack and
is performing routing attacks. We also assume that sensor
nodes can be compromised by an internal attack, but BS can
always be trusted (because no actions of the nodes can ever
compromise BS).
Indirect trust calculations consume additional energy [ 28].
To control this additional consumption, indirect trust calculations are limited to neighbors of a sponsor node (node which
performs trust calculation).

indi,j = Tdi,k × Tdk,j (15)
where Tdi,k denotes the direct trust evaluated by node i for
node k and Tdk,j represents direct trust evaluated by node k for

recommendations is a trusted entity.
However, exchanging recommendations makes a trust model
vulnerable to False Report attacks , where a compromised
neighbor node may send false recommendations about trusted
nodes. To mitigate this vulnerability in our model, a trust
threshold δis used. For simplicity, the degree of trust is represented as a continuous fraction in the range [0, 1]. Every node
is assigned a trust class label by every other node. Table II
shows the classes of nodes according to their trust values.
The initial value of the trust (Trust
initial) between every pair of
nodes in the network is set to 0.5. Node i incorporates recommendations about node j from their common neighbor k only
if node k is a trusted node according to node i, i.e., Tdi,k ≥ δ.
In our algorithm to further improve QoS, nodes labeled as
“trusted” are given higher priority than nodes labeled as “ less
trusted.” In our model, nodes labeled as “ compromised”a r e
totally excluded from the routing path until their trust value
(Trusttotal) ≥ δ.

G. Routing Model
Multipath routing in WSNs is an NP-hard problem due to
its resource-constrained behavior [ 29]. To avoid solving this
problem, a source node should select a routing path by optimizing energy, QoS, and security objectives. Therefore, these
three objectives are combined in a single term known as the
optimized route of the network (ORN)
ORN(i, j) = μ
e ∗TCij + μq ∗QoSij + μs ∗

security factors, respectively, such that 0≤ μe,μ q,μ s ≤ 1 and
μe + μq + μs = 1. The value of ORN(i, j) should be as low
as possible. The inverse value of Trust i,j
total is included because
higher values of Trust i,j
total are desirable.
The objectives to be optimized in (16) are as follows.
1) Lower Energy Consumption: To calculate energy consumption, we consider the cost function given in [ 30].
The energy cost for data transmission from node i to
node j is

)

Equation (18) is used to try ﬁnd the minimum energy
path in the network. Here, if ECi,j + ECj,BS > ECi,BS,
then node i transmits data directly to BS to avoid higher
energy consumption through multihop communication;
otherwise, node i transmits data to node j and so on in
a multihop fashion until it reaches BS.
2) High QoS: To increase QoS, we consider the estimated
hop count and queue delay of a particular node. The QoS
calculation for end-to-end routing delay for a sender
node i, a neighboring node j, and BS is given by
QoS

where hop(j, BS) is the estimated hop count from node
j to BS and qj is the queue delay incurred at node j.W e
initially assume qj = 0.
H. Combining the Models
The relationship between all models is shown in Fig. 2 and
the relationship between components of a WSN using an architecture is shown in Fig. 3. The WSN deployment model is
used ﬁrst to deploy nodes in the FoI, the adaptive connectivity model is used next to deﬁne the R
C values based on
node density and CP. These models are used to mitigate the
energy-hole problem. Next, the communication model is used
to calculate the number of neighbors. Once initialized, the
network tries to detect events. Each time an event is detected
Fig. 2. Relationship between the models.
Fig. 3. Relationship between the components.
by some nodes, such as those in the circle (event area) in
Fig. 3, the adaptive QoS model is used to select the source
node to transmit information to BS. During the selection process, some nodes may be rejected, such as the node with the
“X” mark inside the circle in Fig. 3. After that, the routing
model at the selected source node is used to ﬁnd the appropriate path to BS using the energy model and adaptive security
model. The energy model is used to deﬁne the energy cost
of transmitting the data in the network. The adaptive security
model deﬁnes the security aspects of the network and is used to
calculate the trust values of the nodes in the network. As seen
in Fig. 3, two routing paths that lead to compromised nodes
are rejected because the adaptive security model only selects
nodes with high trust value. The energy model and adaptive
security model are used continuously at each node until the
routing is complete. We assume that routing is only complete
when a notiﬁcation of the event is successfully delivered to
BS. Overall, Fig. 3 shows how our algorithm uses adaptive
QoS and security models to ﬁnd a secure and energy-efﬁcient
routing path.

max-min ant system (MMAS) algorithm [ 31] to ﬁnd the best
route in the network due to its bounded pheromone values,
which increase the convergence speed of the algorithm, i.e.,
lower value of latency.

Our proposed adaptive trust evaluation algorithm (see
Algorithm 1) evaluates direct trust using (14) for each node j
in NR(i) of source node i. For node j, with the help of any node
k that is a common neighbor between neighbor j and source

value is at least the trust threshold, i.e., Tdi,k ≥ δ. Otherwise, if
Tdi,k <δ, node k is removed from the calculation for neighbor
j and another node k is selected. Then, the total trust (Trusti,j

follows: the algorithm starts with the initialization of parameters from Table III. R
C value is set after the nodes are deployed
using (3). Then, each node calculates its set of neighbors NR
using (6) and stores it in their node state. Now, the network
has been initialized and is ready to report events to BS. When
the events are generated in the FoI, they are reported to BS via

that are assigned to ﬁnd a path to BS using information stored
in the node state. After that, the ACO algorithm attempts to
construct routing paths and select min cost path to complete
the data transmission. Here, min cost path is the path with
min cost , as shown in (23). If a suitable path is found, the
algorithm updates the value of the pheromone; otherwise it
constructs more routing paths. Finally, it updates the energy
value and node state for all nodes and marks low-energy nodes
as dead nodes.
V. S
IMULATION RESULTS AND ANALYSIS
In this section, we present simulation results from an
implementation of the LSR algorithm in MATLAB2019b and
network simulator (NS2) [ 33]. In our experiments, we use
NS2 implementation to validate our MATLAB results. Note,
we simulate QEBSR and LSR in MATLAB, and only LSR is
again implemented using NS2 to validate its MATLAB results.
We simulated networks with varying numbers of nodes and
varying percentages of compromised nodes to compare LSR
with QEBSR [20] in terms of network lifetime, PDR, average
routing delay, and energy consumption for trust model calculations. We divided our analysis into three parts, namely, trust
model analysis, network scalability analysis, and security risk
analysis. N nodes are deployed in the FoI, which is a square
region 400 m × 400 m in size. BS is located at the center
of the FoI. We ran each simulation ten times and calculated
the average with a conﬁdence interval of 95%. To initialize
the simulation, the “while (network is operational)” condition
in step 5 of Algorithm 2 was implemented as “while (fewer
than 10% of N nodes are dead with respect to energy).” In our
simulation, an event is not allowed to occur until the previous
one has been handled and an event is skipped if all nearby
Fig. 4. Selection of appropriate values of w1 and w2.
nodes are dead. The average routing delay is deﬁned as the
time taken for a packet to reach BS from the source node.
In this article, we consider the delays required in establishing
paths and routing. The PDR is deﬁned as the ratio between
the number of packets received at BS to the number of packets sent to BS. Table III shows a list of detailed experimental
parameters used in the simulation. The last two parameters in
Table III are varied in the experiments whereas all the other
parameters are ﬁxed during the experiment.
A. Trust Model Analysis
Here, we analyze the performance of our trust model. First,
as shown in Fig. 4, we analyze different values of w
1 and
w2 of (13) in terms of energy consumption (in Joules) for
200 nodes with 10% compromised nodes to calculate trust
values in LSR using NS2 simulation. The constants w1 and
w2 are associated with direct and indirect trust values, respectively. When the values are w1 = 0.4 and w2 = 0.6, there is a
higher energy consumption due to additional weight on indirect trust values. Since indirect trust calculations are limited
to a 1-hop neighbor, giving more weight leads to additional
energy consumption resulting in a lower network lifetime. On
the other hand, the values w
1 = 0.6 and w2 = 0.4 lead to
lower energy consumption and compromise security against
insider attack. Our trust model aims to detect and mitigate the
effect of compromised nodes in advance, leading to a tradeoff between lower energy consumption and a higher level of
security. Therefore, we decided to keep the constant’s value
in (13) as w
1 = 0.5 and w2 = 0.5 for future experiments and
analyses because it balances lower energy consumption and
higher security in the network.
Next, as shown in Fig. 5, we investigate the energy consumption (in Joules) of LSR’s and QEBSR’s trust models.
Fig. 5(a) shows the effect of the varying number of nodes
in the network with 10% compromised nodes, and Fig. 5(b)
shows the effect of the varying percentage of compromised
nodes for a network size of 200 nodes. It is observed that
the overall LSR’s trust model energy consumption is lower
than QEBSR’s trust model, and it is validated with our NS2
implementation. First, the reason for such a trend is direct and
Authorized licensed use limited to: University of Regina IEL. Downloaded on June 16,2025 at 15:34:17 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply.

PATHAK et al.: ADAPTIVE QoS AND TRUST-BASED LIGHTWEIGHT SECURE ROUTING ALGORITHM FOR WSNs 23835
Fig. 5. Energy consumption of trust calculation for varying. (a) Number of nodes. (b) Percentage of compromised nodes.
Fig. 6. Trust value convergence in (a) presence of three nodes and (b) presence of compromised nodes.
indirect trust calculation in our trust model, whereas QEBSR’s
trust model only uses direct trust calculation on the nodes.
Generally, indirect trust calculation results in higher energy
consumption, but our trust model can perform well because
w
1 and w2 are selected to not overburden any component in
(13), and indirect trust calculation is limited to 1-hop neighbors. Second, the routing path calculated by LSR contains
more noncompromised nodes because its trust model that can
mitigate the effect of compromised nodes in advance. Whereas
QEBSR constructs the routing path using only direct trust calculations results in more energy consumption because it can
not guarantee fewer compromised nodes leading to packet drop
by compromised nodes and, recalculation of trust values and
routing paths.
Finally, as shown in Fig. 6, we study the convergence of
trust values in the LSR algorithm using an NS2 simulation.
As shown in Fig. 6(a), we have simulated three nodes with
different behaviors. Node 1 behaves in a trustworthy fashion
for all rounds, Node 2 is compromised for all rounds, and
Node 3 becomes compromised after ten rounds. Note that to
mitigate the effect of compromised nodes in the network, a
trust model must produce a fast decline in those nodes’ trust
values. As anticipated, the trust values change differently for
those behaviors. For all three cases, the initial trust value of
the node is 0.5. The trust value shows an increasing trend
for Node 1 and a decreasing trend for Node 2. Note that the
maximum and minimum trust values are 1 and 0, respectively.
For Node 3, the trust value increases until round 10 (the last
round in which the node behaves in a trustworthy fashion) and
thereafter it decreases. In Fig. 6(b), we simulate a scenario in
which, after gaining the network’s trust (i.e., trust value = 1),
a node becomes compromised. We show the number of rounds
required for LSR’s and QEBSR’s trust models to decrease that
node’s trust value to zero. We observe that LSR’s trust model
drops the trust value to zero in eleven rounds whereas QEBSR
takes 20 rounds to reduce the trust value to zero.
B. Network Scalability Analysis
Here, we analyze the effect of network scalability (increasing number of nodes) in terms of PDR, the average routing
delay, and network lifetime. As shown in Fig. 7, we simulated
Authorized licensed use limited to: University of Regina IEL. Downloaded on June 16,2025 at 15:34:17 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply.

23836 IEEE INTERNET OF THINGS JOURNAL, VOL. 9, NO. 23, 1 DECEMBER 2022
Fig. 7. Effect of varying number of nodes on (a) PDR, (b) average routing delay, and (c) network lifetime.
the network with varying numbers of nodes while keeping the
percentage of the compromised node as 10%. It is observed
that our MATLAB simulation matches the NS2 implementation. Fig. 7(a) shows that PDR increases as the number of
nodes increases in the network. This increase is due to more
nodes in the network successfully delivering the packets from
the event location to BS. The PDR values for LSR is consistently higher than that for QEBSR. The adaptive security
model used in LSR is effective at selecting a secure routing
path by calculating direct and indirect trust values of all the
nodes and excluding nodes labeled as “compromised” from
the routing path. Also, the hybrid deployment model increases
the availability of nodes near BS, which decreases the bottleneck near BS and thus provides multiple routing paths to
BS for a packet. In contrast, QEBSR suffers from an energyhole problem because it uses uniform node deployment. Also,
compromised nodes have larger effects in QEBSR because
the security model relies on a trust model that calculates
only direct trust values with packet transmission and reception rates. Here, our NS2 implementation shows lower values
because it takes in the effect of queue size, due to which there
may be some packet drops. However, the MATLAB and NS2
implementations do not show signiﬁcant differences in their
values.
Fig. 7(b) shows the average routing delay versus the number of nodes. The average routing delay increases with an
increase in the number of nodes because the routing path has
more nodes for a larger network and every node introduces
queuing delays. Overall, LSR has smaller routing delays than
QEBSR. The adaptive QoS model in LSR selects a source
node using the distance to BS and the number of secure neighbors, which decreases delay. Also, the adaptive security model
in LSR mitigates the effect of compromised nodes, with the
help of direct and indirect trust values, which decreases packet
drop and retransmission rates. Due to abundant nodes near BS
in LSR, a packet can be transmitted with a low queuing delay
via multiple nodes. In contrast, QEBSR has less nodes near
BS due to its uniform distribution, which increase the queuing
delay leading to overall increase in routing delay. Here, our
NS2 implementation matches the MATLAB implementation
of the LSR algorithm.
Fig. 7(c) shows a comparison of network lifetime for a varying number of nodes in the network. Overall, an increase
in the number of nodes in the network leads to increased
Fig. 8. Effect of varying network size on network lifetime.
network lifetime for both algorithms. The network lifetime
with QEBSR is less than with LSR due to the following reasons. First, the hybrid deployment and adaptive connectivity
model in LSR help mitigate the energy-hole problem. For
lower node density WSNs (e.g., 100), the effect of the adaptive
connectivity model is greater than that of hybrid deployment
because the value of R
C is calculated to be higher for such
networks. As the node density increases, the value of RC in (2)
decreases. Therefore, for higher node density networks (e.g.,
400), the energy-hole problem is dealt by the hybrid deployment model due to which more nodes are deployed near BS
(e.g., 240), which increases the network lifetime. Second, the
adaptive QoS model results in balanced energy consumption
(due to SS
j parameter) which also leads to increased network
lifetime in the LSR algorithm. Third, recall Figs. 4 and 5(a),
the constants (i.e., w1 and w2) in (13) are selected in such
a way that it reduces the energy consumption in calculating
trust values without compromising security in the network. It
leads to a higher network lifetime for the LSR algorithm than
QEBSR.
In the next experiment, we varied the number of nodes
from 80 to 600 nodes to study the impact of the number of
nodes on the network lifetime (see Fig. 8). As expected from
previous results, the network lifetime for LSR is consistently
higher than for QEBSR, regardless of the number of nodes.
But, for higher numbers of nodes (e.g., 500 and 600 nodes),

Fig. 9. Scenario 1: Effect of Blackhole attack on (a) PDR, (b) average routing delay, and (c) network lifetime.

the network lifetime decreases for both the algorithms. These
results indicate that increasing the number of nodes is not
always an appropriate way to increase network lifetime. A
decrease in a lifetime for bigger networks is due to the extra
energy consumption for communication at nodes used for routing. As more nodes participate in the routing process, more
energy is consumed overall. Therefore, among the scenarios considered in our experiments, the best network size is
between 200 and 400 nodes. Our NS2 implementation again
veriﬁes the simulation results.
In Table IV, we report a network runtime analysis for both
algorithms in MATLAB simulation. The network runtime is
the elapsed time taken for the implemented algorithm until
at least 10% of all nodes are dead. The normalized value is
calculated by converting the network runtime into minutes and
dividing it by its respective network lifetime value. Recording
run time does not inﬂuence the speed of the algorithms. As
shown in the table, the network runtimes for the algorithms
are broadly similar.
C. Security Risk Analysis
Here, we perform security risk analysis by increasing the
percentage of compromised nodes while keeping the total
number of nodes as 200. We investigate the impact on
PDR, average routing delay, and network lifetime under three
scenarios.
In Scenario 1 (Fig. 9), we simulate the LSR and QEBSR
algorithms in the presence of compromised nodes capable
of performing only Blackhole attacks and measure the effect
using PDR, average routing delay, and network lifetime against
an increasing percentage of compromised nodes. Recall that in
Authorized licensed use limited to: University of Regina IEL. Downloaded on June 16,2025 at 15:34:17 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply.

23838 IEEE INTERNET OF THINGS JOURNAL, VOL. 9, NO. 23, 1 DECEMBER 2022
TABLE IV
NETWORK RUNTIME ANALYSIS
a Blackhole attack, a compromised node drops all the received
packets, which deteriorates PDR, average routing delay, and
network lifetime. As the percentage of compromised nodes
increases, we observe a decrease in PDR [Fig. 9(a)] and
network lifetime [Fig. 9(c)], and an increase in average routing
delay [Fig. 9(b)]. A trust model is more secure and effective,
i.e., can rapidly mitigate the inﬂuence of compromised nodes
from the routing paths, if it quickly reduce trust values of
the compromised nodes in the network. LSR can decrease
the trust value of nodes compromised by Blackhole attacks
much faster than QEBSR, as shown in Fig. 6(b). When a node
near BS gets compromised, retransmission of packets from the
source node increases because a compromised node drops all
received packets and does not allow them to reach BS. LSR
can mitigate this situation because the algorithm contains a
trust calculation model that is faster in detecting compromised
nodes, an adaptive connectivity model that calculates appropriate R
C values to ensure high connectivity between nodes, and
a hybrid deployment model that deploys more nodes near BS.
In contrast, QEBSR lacks all three models, which affects PDR,
average routing delay, and network lifetime as the percentage
of compromised nodes in the network increases.
In Scenario 2 (Fig. 10), we simulate the LSR and QEBSR
algorithms in the presence of compromised nodes capable of
performing only Flooding attacks and measure the effect using
PDR, average routing delay, and network lifetime against an
increasing percentage of compromised nodes. In a Flooding
attack, a compromised node ﬂoods the network with packets,
resulting in packet collisions and retransmission of packets by
trusted nodes. We observe that PDR, average routing delay,
and network lifetime are more degraded in Flooding attacks
than in Blackhole attacks. LSR and QEBSR try to avoid choosing compromised nodes for the routing path. However, we
observe that LSR, using its adaptive security model, adaptive
connectivity model, and hybrid deployment model, performs
better than QEBSR in terms of PDR, average routing delay,
and network lifetime.
In Scenario 3 (Fig. 11), we simulate a more realistic attack
model for the LSR and QEBSR algorithms. In this scenario,
compromised nodes are capable of performing either of the
two attacks (Blackhole and Flooding) in a round. For simplicity, we assume a compromised node randomly performs either
a Flooding attack or a Blackhole attack on the entire network
with an equal likelihood in each round. The performance
metrics (PDR, average routing delay, and network lifetime) are
consistently worse in a network with combined attacks than
in a network with only one kind of attack (Figs. 9 and 10).
However, LSR performs better than QEBSR in all metrics.
LSR can better mitigate the effects of a compromised node in
the routing path using its adaptive security model. As well, its
hybrid deployment model deploys more nodes near BS, which
can provide more trusted nodes in the vicinity of BS. These
two differences contribute to a higher rate of packet delivery
than with QEBSR.
VI. C
ONCLUSION
In this article, we described the multiobjective WSN
optimization problem. We proposed the LSR algorithm to
simultaneously mitigate the energy-hole problem and increase
coverage, energy efﬁciency, QoS, and security. Our algorithm
consists of four models, namely, the hybrid deployment model,
the dynamic connectivity model, the adaptive QoS model, and
the adaptive security model. Our hybrid deployment model
used 2-D Gaussian distribution to deploy 60% of the nodes
around BS to mitigate the energy-hole problem for high node
density network and 40% of the nodes deployed using uniform distribution to provide high coverage around the edges
of the FoI. Our dynamic connectivity model calculated R
C
value using CP and node density to mitigate the energyhole problem for low node density networks. Our adaptive
QoS model selected a source node based on four parameters
related to residual energy, delay, security, and SSJ (the number of times node j has acted as a source node in previous
rounds). Our adaptive security model improved the detection
of compromised nodes in the network using direct and indirect trust calculations. Using these models, we observed that
our proposed LSR algorithm improved PDR, average routing
delay, network lifetime, energy consumption for calculating
trust values, and trust value convergence compared to the
existing QEBSR technique for varying number of nodes and
percentage of compromised nodes. We performed our simulation in MATLAB and validated the results using NS2
simulation. We also showed the inﬂuence of varying the number of nodes on the network lifetime and determined the best
network size is between 200 and 400 nodes. We reported
the network runtime analysis for both the algorithms. In
addition, the results and analysis showed that LSR decreased
the chance of packet dropping and retransmission due to
its adaptive security model. Also, LSR resulted in balanced
Authorized licensed use limited to: University of Regina IEL. Downloaded on June 16,2025 at 15:34:17 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply.

PATHAK et al.: ADAPTIVE QoS AND TRUST-BASED LIGHTWEIGHT SECURE ROUTING ALGORITHM FOR WSNs 23839
energy consumption and a higher network lifetime, which
makes it suitable for deployment in a hostile environment.
Future research could improve the LSR algorithm to address
three technical challenges. First, LSR assumes a rectangular FoI. To allow wider application, the algorithm could be
extended to cover diverse shapes of FoIs (e.g., circular or
hexagon). Second, in our adaptive security model, the indirect
trust value calculation is limited to 1-hop neighbors because
indirect trust calculation consumes additional energy. This
challenge could be addressed by implementing our security
model in edge nodes (edge computing). Since edge nodes
have more resources, indirect trust calculation can be extended
to a multihop scenario. Third, if any sensor node is compromised, the event data stored inside that node could be
captured by an adversary, which would lead to a breach in
data security. To address this challenge, one could improve
data security by implementing a blockchain-based trust model.
Since a blockchain is an immutable ledger, storing the event
data in a blockchain would increase the data security.

Aditya Pathak (Graduate Student Member, IEEE)
received the B.E. degree in electronic and communication engineering from Gujarat Technological
University, Ahmedabad, India, in 2017, and the
M.A.Sc. degree in electronic systems engineering
from the University of Regina, Regina, SK, Canada,
in 2021, where he is currently pursuing the Ph.D.
degree in electronic systems engineering.
His research area includes blockchain, Internet of
Things, edge computing, trust-based security, and
joint optimization of security, Quality of Service,
and energy consumption.
Mr. Pathak was the Past Student activities Chair for IEEE South
Saskatchewan section.

Irfan Al-Anbagi (Senior Member, IEEE) received
the Ph.D. degree in electrical and computer engineering from the University of Ottawa, Ottawa, ON,
Canada, in October 2013.
He is an Associate Professor with the Faculty
of Engineering and Applied Science, University of
Regina, Regina, SK, Canada. From 2013 to 2015,
he worked as a Postdoctoral Fellow and a Product
Development Manager of the “SecCharge” Project
with the University of Ottawa. His research activities
focus on Internet of Things systems, wireless sensor
networks (WSN), smart grid communication and information systems, including big-data analysis, system reliability and optimization, and cybersecurity.
Dr. Al-Anbagi’s journal article titled “A Survey on Cross-Layer Quality of
Service Approaches in WSNs for Delay and Reliability Aware Applications,”
published in IEEE C
OMMUNICATIONS SURVEYS AND TUTORIALS ,w o nt h e
2017 Best Paper Award from the IEEE Communications Society’s Technical
Committee on Communications Systems Integration and Modeling. He is
registered as a Professional Engineer with the Association of Professional
Engineers and Geoscientists of Saskatchewan and Professional Engineers
Ontario. He is the past Chair of the IEEE South Saskatchewan section.
Howard J. Hamilton received the B.Sc. and
M.Sc. degrees in computational science from the
University of Saskatchewan, Saskatoon, SK, Canada,
in 1980 and 1983, respectively, and the Ph.D. degree
in computing science from Simon Fraser University,
Burnaby, BC, Canada, in 1992.
He is a Professor with the Department of
Computer Science, University of Regina, Regina,
SK, Canada, where he has served since 1991. He is
the Director of the University of Regina’s Institute
for Computational Discovery. He has coauthored
Knowledge Discovery and Measures of Interest and coedited four other books,
including Quality Measures for Data Mining . His research interests include
machine learning, data mining, blockchains, applying artiﬁcial intelligence to
computer animation and computer games, and spatio-temporal data mining.
